name: Thunderforge (DoD/DIU, 2025)
origin_domain: military AI (U.S. Department of Defense / Defense Innovation Unit agentic AI program)
year_developed: 2025 (contract awarded March 5, 2025; MVP testing with INDOPACOM June 2025; full operational capability planned 2028+)
progenitor: Defense Innovation Unit (DIU), U.S. Department of Defense; Prime Contractor: Scale AI; Key Partners: Microsoft (LLM infrastructure), Anduril (Lattice platform for modeling and simulation), CMU Software Engineering Institute (AI trustworthiness and evaluation)
driving_insight: Thunderforge's driving insight is that the U.S. military's operational planning and wargaming processes are bottlenecked by human cognitive throughput — legacy planning tools and processes cannot keep pace with the speed of modern peer-adversary warfare, where sensor-to-shooter timelines have collapsed from hours to seconds. The program's central bet is that AI agents can serve as 'digital staff officers' that augment rather than replace human planners — each AI agent specializes in a domain (logistics, intelligence, cyber, information operations) and critiques or generates war plans in parallel, producing analyses that would take human staff weeks or months in hours or minutes. The second-order insight is about trust architecture: Thunderforge explicitly positions humans 'on the loop' rather than 'in the loop' — the AI generates options and critiques, the human commander applies strategic judgment to AI-generated outputs and makes the final decision. This is a deliberate design choice to maintain human accountability while exploiting AI's speed and breadth of analysis. The third-order insight is about iteration: Thunderforge aims to create a 'flywheel' where AI-generated wargame outcomes feed back into plan refinement, enabling thousands of scenario iterations where human-only processes could manage dozens. This represents a fundamental shift from 'plan, then wargame once or twice' to 'continuously plan and wargame in parallel at machine speed.'
decision_style: hierarchical with human-on-the-loop AI augmentation. The human commander retains ultimate decision-making authority — AI agents generate options, critiques, and wargame outcomes, but humans decide. The decision architecture has three tiers: (1) AI agents make analytical decisions (identifying plan weaknesses, generating COAs, predicting wargame outcomes) using LLM-based reasoning; (2) A fusion or orchestrator agent (or human staff) synthesizes multiple agent outputs into a coherent assessment; (3) The human commander makes the final operational decision based on AI-generated options and their own strategic judgment. This is not algorithmic decision-making (the AI is not making engage/no-engage decisions) and not emergent or vote-based — it is a hierarchical model where AI augments each level of the existing military decision hierarchy. The 'on the loop' (vs. 'in the loop') distinction is critical: being 'in the loop' means the human must approve every AI action before it executes (serial bottleneck); being 'on the loop' means the AI operates independently under human oversight, and the human intervenes when judgment is required or when the AI flags a decision for human review (parallel acceleration). Thunderforge is designed for the latter model, though its current MVP phase operates closer to 'in the loop' for safety.
problem_class_targeted: Thunderforge targets operational-level military planning and wargaming characterized by: (1) information overload — the volume of intelligence, logistics, and operational data exceeds human analytical capacity; (2) time compression — planning timelines that historically took weeks must be compressed to hours or minutes to keep pace with adversary decision cycles; (3) multi-domain complexity — plans must account for land, air, maritime, space, and cyber effects simultaneously; (4) adversarial adaptation — the enemy is actively trying to defeat Blue plans and will exploit overlooked weaknesses; (5) scenario explosion — the number of possible adversary COAs and environmental contingencies is combinatorially vast and cannot be exhaustively explored by human planners; (6) distributed expertise — effective planning requires deep expertise in multiple domains, which no single human planner possesses. The problem class is ill-defined in the sense that there is no single 'correct' plan, dynamic (adversary adapts), and adversarial. Thunderforge does not target tactical execution (it is not an autonomous weapon system) — it targets the planning and wargaming phases that precede execution.
ordered_phases: Thunderforge's operational architecture has two development tracks with distinct phase structures. TRACK 1 — Cognitive Planning and Red-Teaming: (1) INPUT — a human-authored war plan (or AI-generated draft plan) is submitted to the Thunderforge system. (2) DECOMPOSE — the plan is decomposed into domain-specific aspects (logistics, intelligence, cyber, fires, maneuver, sustainment, information operations) and distributed to specialized AI agents. (3) ANALYZE — each AI agent analyzes its domain aspect, identifying strengths, weaknesses, assumptions, gaps, and risks. Agents operate in parallel. (4) FUSE — agent outputs are fused into a consolidated red-team assessment, highlighting cross-domain interactions and compounding risks that no single agent would identify alone. (5) REVIEW — the fused assessment is presented to the human commander and staff for review, discussion, and decision. (6) ITERATE — based on human feedback, the plan is revised and re-analyzed, iterating until the commander is satisfied. TRACK 2 — Modeling, Simulation, and Scenario Generation: (1) SCENARIO GENERATION — LLMs generate vast numbers of realistic military scenarios derived from DOD-validated simulation tools (e.g., DARPA SAFE-SiM). (2) SIMULATION — AI agents simulate Blue and Red force decision-making within each scenario, running thousands of parallel wargames. (3) OUTCOME ANALYSIS — AI analyzes simulation outcomes, identifying patterns, critical decision points, and plan vulnerabilities. (4) FEEDBACK — analysis results feed back into Track 1, informing plan revision and COA development. The two tracks are designed to operate concurrently, with Track 1 producing the plan and Track 2 stress-testing it.
feedback_loops: Thunderforge is designed around multiple feedback loops. (1) Red-team → Plan revision loop (Track 1 internal): AI red-team analysis feeds back into plan revision, creating an iterative improvement cycle that converges toward a robust plan. (2) Wargame → Plan validation loop (Track 2 → Track 1): simulation outcomes reveal plan weaknesses that drive plan revision. (3) Real-world → Model update loop: operational experience (actual operations, not just wargames) feeds back into the simulation models, improving their fidelity over time (planned for post-2026). (4) Human-in-the-loop feedback: the human commander's decisions and overrides provide supervised feedback that can be used to fine-tune the AI agents' analytical judgment (planned for later phases). (5) Cross-agent feedback: when one domain agent identifies an issue that affects another domain (e.g., a logistics constraint that limits maneuver options), the fusion layer propagates this constraint to other agents for re-analysis. All loops are explicitly designed into the system architecture. The flywheel concept (wargame outcomes improve plans, better plans produce better wargame outcomes, iterating at machine speed) is the system's central design metaphor.
gates: Thunderforge's gate structure is designed around the human-on-the-loop philosophy. (1) Plan submission gate: before AI analysis begins, the human-authored plan must meet baseline quality standards (completeness, format compliance) — this is a human gate. (2) AI analysis → Human review gate: AI-generated red-team assessments and COA recommendations do not automatically modify the plan; they pass through a human review gate where the commander and staff assess the AI's findings. (3) COA selection gate: if the AI generates multiple COAs, the human commander selects which to pursue — this is the most critical gate, directly mapping to the JTC's Phase 4 Commander's Decision. (4) Wargame → Plan modification gate: simulation results that suggest plan changes require human approval before the plan is modified — the AI does not auto-modify operational plans. (5) Safety gate: if the AI generates a recommendation that violates standing ROE, policy constraints, or exhibits known AI failure modes (hallucination, escalation bias), the system flags or blocks the recommendation before it reaches the commander. If a gate blocks: recommendations are returned to the AI for revision with human feedback, or discarded if fundamentally flawed. The system is designed so that AI output is always advisory, never automatically authoritative — the gates enforce this constraint.
exit_criteria: Thunderforge's planning process exits when: (1) the human commander approves the plan (the plan is 'good enough' for the operational requirement); OR (2) the planning timeline expires (a plan must be delivered by a deadline, and further iteration is not possible); OR (3) the AI-human iteration converges (successive iterations produce diminishing improvements, indicating the plan has reached the quality ceiling achievable with current information). The 'done' state is a human judgment call, not an algorithmic determination — the commander decides when the plan is sufficiently robust. For wargaming (Track 2), exit criteria are: (a) the wargame campaign has explored a statistically significant sample of scenarios; (b) key vulnerabilities have been identified and addressed; (c) the marginal value of additional wargame runs has dropped below a threshold. Thunderforge is designed for continuous operation — it doesn't truly 'exit' but rather cycles between planning (Track 1), wargaming (Track 2), and replanning based on real-world developments.
decomposition_method: Thunderforge decomposes the planning problem using domain-driven decomposition: the overall war plan is decomposed into domain-specific aspects (logistics, intelligence, cyber, fires, maneuver, sustainment, information operations) and each aspect is assigned to a specialized AI agent. This is fundamentally a functional decomposition: each agent owns a warfighting function or staff section responsibility. The decomposition mirrors the existing military staff structure (J1 through J9), making it intuitive for military users. Within each domain, further decomposition may occur (e.g., the logistics agent may decompose into fuel, ammunition, medical, transportation sub-problems). The decomposition is not purely MECE — there are intentional overlaps (e.g., both the fires agent and the cyber agent analyze enemy air defense suppression) because AI agents can productively cross-check each other's work. The fusion layer synthesizes overlapping analyses and resolves contradictions. This is a value-driven decomposition: each domain agent provides a perspective that a single generalist agent could not provide as deeply, and the fusion of perspectives produces insights that no single perspective could generate.
granularity_guidance: Thunderforge's granularity is still being defined through operational testing, but the design intent is to decompose to the level of individual plan assumptions, COA branches, and risk assessments. A leaf node for Thunderforge analysis would be a specific claim in a war plan that the AI can independently verify or challenge (e.g., 'the logistics estimate assumes Port X can throughput Y tons per day; is this supported by historical data and current intelligence?'). The stopping condition is pragmatic: decompose until each analytical claim can be independently verified by an AI agent with access to relevant data sources. The system does not decompose to the level of individual tactical engagements — it operates at the operational level, leaving tactical granularity to existing C2 systems. Granularity guidance is expected to evolve as the system is tested with operational users.
boundary_rules: Thunderforge enforces boundaries between AI agents through: (1) Domain scope — each agent's system prompt and tool access is scoped to its assigned domain, preventing one agent from making claims about another domain's assessments without cross-referencing. (2) Fusion layer deconfliction — the fusion agent (or human staff) identifies and resolves conflicting assessments from different domain agents. (3) Human review boundary — AI agents cannot modify the operational plan directly; they can only produce assessments and recommendations that humans review. (4) Data access boundaries — agents have access only to data relevant to their domain, enforced through access controls on the underlying data stores (not all data is available to all agents). (5) Air-gapped deployment — in operational environments, Thunderforge will run on classified networks with strict data boundary enforcement; AI agents cannot exfiltrate data to unclassified systems. (6) The boundary rules are still maturing — current MVP testing is establishing which boundaries work in practice and which create unproductive coordination overhead.
risk_assessment_mechanism: Thunderforge's risk assessment is multi-layered and AI-augmented. (1) AI red-teaming: each domain agent identifies risks specific to its domain (e.g., logistics agent identifies supply chain single-points-of-failure). (2) Cross-domain risk synthesis: the fusion layer identifies compounding risks where failures in multiple domains interact (e.g., logistics failure combined with cyber attack on C2 creates a catastrophic risk that neither domain agent would flag alone). (3) Wargame-driven risk discovery: Track 2 simulations reveal risks that human planners and individual AI agents missed by exploring thousands of scenarios where those risks materialize. (4) Known AI risk catalog: the CMU SEI is developing a catalog of known AI failure modes (hallucination, escalation bias, recency bias, country-specific bias) and auditing Thunderforge outputs for these patterns. (5) Confidence calibration: the system is designed to associate confidence scores with AI-generated assessments, enabling the human commander to distinguish high-confidence from low-confidence findings. (6) The risk assessment mechanism is probabilistic and LLM-judged, with human validation for high-consequence risks.
unknown_handling: When Thunderforge encounters unknowns, it follows a graduated response: (1) Data gaps — if an AI agent needs data that is not available (e.g., adversary logistics capacity is unknown), the agent flags the gap and either proceeds with explicitly stated assumptions (marked as uncertain) or defers the analysis until data is available. (2) Model uncertainty — if the AI agent is unsure about its own analysis (low confidence), it flags the analysis as uncertain and may request human SME review. (3) Out-of-distribution scenarios — if the wargame simulation encounters a scenario that is fundamentally different from anything in the training data, the AI may produce unreliable outputs; the system is designed to detect such scenarios through confidence scoring and escalate to human review. (4) Adversary unknowns — the system models adversary behavior probabilistically, acknowledging that the adversary's actual COA may differ from the modeled COAs. (5) The system does not halt on unknowns — it proceeds with explicitly flagged assumptions and uncertainties, allowing the human commander to decide whether the residual uncertainty is acceptable. This is a 'default-permissive with explicit flagging' approach, appropriate for the planning domain where perfect information is never available. (6) For safety-critical unknowns (e.g., the AI is unsure whether a proposed COA violates ROE), the default is restrictive: block and escalate to human review.
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE with explicit flagging for analytical unknowns; RESTRICTIVE for legal/policy/safety unknowns. This dual stance reflects Thunderforge's operational domain: in planning, acting on incomplete information with acknowledged uncertainty is standard practice (every plan is based on assumptions); but in legal and safety matters, the cost of being wrong is catastrophic, so the system defaults to blocking until human review confirms compliance. The permissive stance on analytical unknowns is calibrated by confidence scoring: low-confidence assessments are flagged prominently so the commander does not mistake AI uncertainty for AI confidence.
safety_guardrails: Thunderforge's safety guardrails are a central design concern, reflecting the high-stakes operational domain. (1) Human-on-the-loop architecture: AI agents produce recommendations and analyses; humans make decisions. This is the primary safety guardrail — the AI cannot autonomously execute plan changes. (2) Output explainability: AI agents must provide traceable reasoning (chain-of-evidence) for their assessments, enabling human verification. (3) Adversarial testing: the system is tested against adversarial inputs (deliberately misleading intelligence, plans with hidden flaws) to evaluate whether AI agents detect or propagate errors. (4) Escalation bias monitoring: studies have shown that LLMs tend toward escalatory military recommendations; Thunderforge includes explicit monitoring for escalation bias and flags escalatory recommendations. (5) Formal verification: CMU SEI is exploring formal verification methods to mathematically prove that AI behavior remains within acceptable bounds for specific, well-defined sub-tasks. (6) Red teaming: dedicated AI red teams continuously probe the system for vulnerabilities (prompt injection, lies-in-the-loop, tool misuse, bias exploitation). (7) Air-gapped deployment: Thunderforge operates on classified networks isolated from the internet, reducing external attack surface. (8) Kill switch: human operators can disable AI agent operations at any time. (9) Model selection: the program selected GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet as base models partly because studies showed these models are notably more restrained in military escalation scenarios than alternatives (DeepSeek V3, Qwen2, Llama 8B). (10) Sandboxed wargaming: Track 2 simulations run in sandboxed environments where AI-generated adversarial behaviors cannot affect real-world systems.
human_decision_points: Humans are ON the loop for analytical work and IN the loop for decision-making. Specific human decision points: (1) Plan submission — a human must submit the initial plan (or authorize AI-generated plan drafts). (2) COA selection — if the AI generates multiple courses of action, the human commander selects which to pursue. (3) Risk acceptance — the human commander decides whether the residual risk identified by AI red-teaming is acceptable. (4) Plan approval — the final plan requires human commander approval. (5) Wargame scenario parameters — humans set the parameters for wargaming campaigns (adversary capabilities, ROE, political constraints). (6) System override — humans can pause, redirect, or halt any AI analytical process. (7) Model update authorization — humans decide whether to incorporate operational feedback into model fine-tuning. The 'on the loop' distinction means that the AI operates independently (generating analyses, running wargames, producing options) while the human monitors and intervenes when judgment is required, rather than the human approving every intermediate AI action. This is the core architectural bet: that AI can be trusted to operate independently for analytical tasks while humans remain the authority for consequential decisions.
override_capability: Override capability is comprehensive and is a core safety requirement. (1) Full halt — human operators can stop all AI agent operations. (2) Per-agent halt — specific AI agents can be disabled while others continue. (3) Plan override — the human commander can reject AI-generated assessments and COAs and direct a different approach. (4) Data override — humans can correct AI data inputs if the AI is operating on incorrect information. (5) Output filtering — human staff can filter AI outputs before they reach the commander, removing irrelevant or low-quality analyses. (6) Override granularity ranges from strategic (redirect the entire planning effort) to tactical (correct a specific factual error in an AI assessment). (7) All overrides are logged for audit and learning purposes. The override architecture is designed to ensure that AI augmentation does not become AI compulsion — the human remains in command, and AI is a tool that can be overridden at any level.
fallback_when_no_human: Thunderforge is designed for continuous human availability during operational use — it is not designed for fully autonomous planning without human oversight. If the human commander is unavailable: (1) AI analytical processes continue (ongoing wargames, ongoing red-team analyses) to produce results for when the commander returns; (2) No plan modifications are auto-approved — the system queues recommendations for human review; (3) Pre-authorized analytical tasks continue (e.g., scheduled wargaming campaigns), but the outputs are held for human review; (4) Time-critical decisions escalate to the next available human in the chain of command, not to the AI. The fallback is conservative: AI continues analytical work in the background but does not make or communicate decisions. This reflects the current state of DOD policy — there is no authorization for autonomous AI decision-making in operational planning, and Thunderforge is not designed to push that boundary.
max_problem_size: Thunderforge has been demonstrated at INDOPACOM tabletop exercise scale (June 2025 MVP), which involves theater-level planning for the Indo-Pacific region — the largest area of responsibility in the U.S. military, spanning 36 nations and 50% of the world's population. The target scale is all 11 U.S. combatant commands plus NATO and Five Eyes integration. The theoretical limit is constrained by: (1) LLM context windows — war plans and associated intelligence can exceed even the largest context windows (1M-2M tokens); (2) cross-agent coordination complexity — as the number of specialized agents increases, the fusion layer's coordination overhead grows; (3) data availability — AI agents can only analyze what data is digitized and accessible; much military data remains in legacy, non-machine-readable formats. What breaks at scale: (a) the human commander's cognitive bandwidth — even AI-summarized analyses become overwhelming if the volume of AI-generated content exceeds the human's absorption capacity; (b) data security — scaling to all 11 combatant commands requires cross-classification data sharing that is technically and policy-complex.
parallel_execution: Parallelism is a first-class design principle — Thunderforge's architecture is fundamentally parallel. (1) Multiple AI domain agents analyze the same plan concurrently (Track 1). (2) Multiple wargame scenarios run in parallel (Track 2) — the system is designed to run thousands of concurrent simulations. (3) Tracks 1 and 2 operate concurrently — planning and wargaming are not sequential phases but parallel processes that inform each other. (4) Agent-to-agent communication enables parallel cross-domain analysis without serial handoffs. (5) Dependency resolution: the fusion layer resolves dependencies between parallel analyses, ensuring that when Agent A's logistics analysis reveals a constraint relevant to Agent B's maneuver analysis, Agent B is updated. (6) The parallelism is cloud-scale: Microsoft provides the LLM infrastructure supporting concurrent model inference across many agents. The system's ability to run more concurrent analyses than human staff is its primary value proposition.
team_scaling_model: Thunderforge's team scaling is fundamentally different from human team scaling because AI agents can be replicated at low marginal cost. (1) Adding more AI agents (more domain specializations, more parallel wargame runs) increases analytical breadth with approximately linear marginal cost in compute. (2) The fusion layer's coordination overhead grows with the number of agents, potentially creating sub-linear or negative returns beyond an optimal agent count (likely 7-15 specialized agents based on military staff structure). (3) However, because AI agents lack the coordination overhead of human teams (no meetings, no shift changes, no interpersonal friction), the team scaling model is fundamentally more favorable than human team scaling. (4) The primary scaling constraint is the human commander's cognitive capacity to absorb AI-generated outputs — if the AI generates more analysis than the human can process, the additional analytical capacity adds cost without adding value. This is a recognized scaling challenge that the program is addressing through improved summarization and prioritization of AI outputs.
cost_scaling_model: Thunderforge's computational cost scales roughly linearly with the number of AI analyses performed (each analysis requires LLM inference, which scales with input and output tokens). Key cost drivers: (1) LLM inference — the dominant cost, scaling with the number of agents, the complexity of plans being analyzed, and the number of wargame iterations; (2) Simulation compute (Track 2) — DOD-validated physics-based simulations are computationally intensive independent of the AI layer; (3) Data infrastructure — maintaining classified cloud infrastructure for multi-command operations. The cost is front-loaded on infrastructure (classified cloud, data pipelines, integration) and variable on operational use (per-plan analysis, per-wargame run). The branching factor in Track 1 is low (each plan revision generates one re-analysis cycle); in Track 2, the branching factor is configurable (the number of wargame runs is a parameter set by operators). Scale AI's contract is described as 'multimillion-dollar' — exact financial terms are not publicly disclosed. The program's economic rationale is that AI analytical cost is dramatically lower than the equivalent human staff cost for the same analytical throughput.
replanning_built_in: Thunderforge is designed for continuous replanning — it is not a one-shot planning tool. (1) Track 1's iterative cycle (plan → AI red-team → review → revise → re-analyze) inherently supports replanning until the commander is satisfied. (2) Track 2's continuous wargaming enables the system to detect when a previously validated plan becomes vulnerable due to changed conditions and flag it for replanning. (3) Replanning can be local (a specific branch or assumption is updated) or global (the entire plan is re-analyzed). (4) The system is designed to support both pre-execution replanning (during the planning phase) and during-execution replanning (adapting the plan as operations unfold), though the latter is a future capability (post-2026). (5) Replanning is a first-class feature, not an afterthought — the system's entire value proposition is enabling faster, more thorough replanning than human-only processes.
trigger_mechanism: Thunderforge's replanning triggers include: (1) Red-team findings — AI analysis identifies a critical plan weakness that requires revision. (2) Wargame outcomes — simulation reveals that the plan fails against a specific adversary COA above a threshold probability. (3) Intelligence updates — new intelligence changes the planning assumptions (e.g., adversary capability discovered that was not in the original plan's threat model). (4) Commander direction — the human commander requests a plan revision based on their judgment or new guidance from higher headquarters. (5) Timeline triggers — periodic plan reviews at scheduled intervals (e.g., weekly plan refresh). (6) Environmental triggers — detected changes in political, diplomatic, or operational conditions. Triggers are both AI-detected (the system identifies a condition that warrants replanning) and human-initiated (the commander orders replanning). The integration of AI-detected triggers represents a capability leap over traditional planning, where condition detection relies entirely on human staff.
adaptation_cost: Thunderforge's adaptation cost is designed to be dramatically lower than human-only replanning. (1) AI re-analysis of a revised plan is cheap (LLM inference cost for analyzing the delta vs. re-analyzing the entire plan — the FRAGO principle applies). (2) Wargame re-runs to validate a plan revision are computationally cheap relative to the cost of human-conducted wargames. (3) The primary adaptation cost is the human commander's time to review AI-generated analyses and make decisions — this human cognitive cost is the bottleneck that Thunderforge cannot eliminate. (4) Lost work is minimal — AI analyses of plan elements that haven't changed remain valid and don't need to be re-run. (5) State preservation: the system maintains the full analysis history, wargame results, and plan version history, enabling efficient incremental replanning. The cost advantage over human-only planning is orders of magnitude for analytical throughput; the remaining cost is human decision time, which is irreducible.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Thunderforge's failure recovery mechanisms are still being developed, but the design includes: (1) AI error detection — cross-agent comparison can identify when one agent's analysis contradicts others, flagging potential AI hallucination or error for human review. (2) Wargame validation — if wargaming contradicts an AI's analytical assessment, the discrepancy is flagged. (3) Confidence calibration — low-confidence AI outputs are flagged for human review rather than being passed to the commander as authoritative. (4) Human-in-the-loop recovery — the ultimate failure recovery mechanism is the human commander, who can reject AI analyses that don't make sense and direct re-analysis. (5) Graceful degradation — if an AI agent fails (producing nonsensical or empty output), other agents continue operating, and the failed agent's domain analysis is either performed by a backup agent or flagged as a gap. (6) Blast radius containment — AI agents operate in sandboxed environments; a failure in one agent does not corrupt other agents or the underlying plan data. (7) Rollback — plan version management enables reverting to a previous plan version if AI-driven plan changes prove problematic.
output_artifacts: Thunderforge produces: (1) Red-team assessment reports — structured critiques of war plans identifying strengths, weaknesses, assumptions, and risks across all domains. (2) Courses of Action (COAs) — AI-generated alternative approaches with comparative analysis. (3) Wargame outcome reports — statistical summaries of simulation results showing plan performance across thousands of scenarios. (4) Risk matrices — consolidated risk assessments with confidence scores, severity ratings, and identified mitigations. (5) Plan revision recommendations — specific, actionable suggestions for plan improvement with rationale. (6) Intelligence and logistics summaries — domain-specific analyses that human staff can use as inputs to their own planning. (7) Audit trails — records of all AI analyses, decisions, and human overrides for accountability and learning. These artifacts are designed to integrate with existing military planning products (OPORDs, JIPTLs, CDE products) rather than replace them.
briefing_format: Thunderforge is designed to integrate with existing military briefing formats while adding AI-specific elements. (1) Commander's Decision Brief — the traditional briefing format augmented with AI-generated sections (risk assessment, alternative COAs, wargame results). (2) AI confidence dashboard — a visual display showing which assessments are high-confidence (supported by multiple agents and wargame validation) vs. low-confidence (single agent, limited data, contradictory signals). (3) Narrative summaries — AI-generated plain-language summaries of complex analyses for commander consumption. (4) Interactive drill-down — the commander can ask the AI to elaborate on specific findings during the briefing, creating an interactive rather than static briefing experience. (5) The compression format is designed to be commander-centric: the commander sees 'here's what's important, here's what's uncertain, here's what I need you to decide' rather than the full analytical depth.
traceability: Thunderforge is designed for strong traceability, recognizing that combatant command decisions must be auditable. (1) Chain-of-evidence reasoning: AI agents are required to provide traceable reasoning for their assessments — linking claims to the data and assumptions they're based on. (2) Decision record: all human decisions (COA selection, risk acceptance, plan approval) are logged with the AI-generated information that informed them. (3) Audit trail: the system maintains a complete log of AI analyses, wargame runs, human overrides, and plan versions. (4) Model version traceability: the specific AI model version used for each analysis is recorded, recognizing that model updates may change outputs. (5) This traceability is essential for both legal accountability (in the event of a plan failure, the decision-making process must be reconstructable) and system improvement (identifying when and why AI analyses were wrong). Traceability is a core design requirement, not an afterthought.
agent_communication_protocol: Thunderforge uses a proprietary architecture integrating Scale AI's agentic platform, Microsoft's LLM infrastructure, and Anduril's Lattice platform. Inter-agent communication uses custom protocols within the Scale AI framework — not standard public protocols like MCP or A2A. Military system integration uses existing military protocols (Link 16, JADOCS, etc.) for connecting to operational C2 systems. In concept, the communication architecture is: (1) a central message bus through which all agents communicate; (2) structured message formats for plan data, analytical assessments, and wargame results; (3) synchronous communication for real-time analytical collaboration and asynchronous communication for long-running wargames; (4) agent discovery through a registry (agents are pre-defined, not dynamically discovered). The commercial AI components (Microsoft LLMs) are accessed via API within the classified cloud environment. The architecture is designed for classified, air-gapped deployment, which imposes constraints not present in civilian agent systems.
learning_memory_architecture: Thunderforge's learning architecture is planned for phased development. Current/Phase 1 (2025): (1) No online learning — AI agents do not learn from operational use; their knowledge is static (frozen model weights + retrieval-augmented generation from approved data sources). (2) Episodic memory — the system stores analysis histories, wargame results, and plan versions for retrieval in future planning sessions, but does not use them to update model behavior. Planned/Phase 2 (2026+): (1) Post-hoc fine-tuning — using human feedback on AI analyses (which analyses were judged correct/useful vs. incorrect/misleading) to fine-tune agent models. (2) Skill library — developing a library of validated analytical patterns (common plan weaknesses, common adversary responses) that agents can reference. (3) Distillation — compacting lessons learned from wargame outcomes into more efficient models. Phase 3 (2028+): (1) Operational learning from real-world plan outcomes (not just wargames). (2) Continuous model improvement from operational feedback. The learning architecture is conservative by design — DOD is appropriately cautious about AI systems that learn from operational data without human oversight.
latency_bounds: Thunderforge operates at interactive latency for analytical tasks (seconds to minutes for a single agent to analyze a plan section) and batch latency for wargaming (hours for thousands of simulation runs). The target latency for Track 1 red-teaming is 'minutes to hours' (replacing a human process that takes 'days to weeks'). The target latency for COA generation is 'interactive' — the commander should be able to ask the system 'what if we tried this approach?' and receive an AI-generated analysis within seconds to minutes. Degradation under time pressure: if the commander needs an answer in seconds, the system provides a rapid analysis based on fewer agents, fewer wargame iterations, and higher tolerance for uncertainty — analogous to the F2T2EA dynamic targeting time compression. The system is designed to degrade gracefully under time pressure, providing a coarser but faster analysis when time is critical.
evaluation_benchmarking: Thunderforge is too early in its development for standardized benchmarks. The program's evaluation framework (under development by CMU SEI) includes: (1) Human expert comparison — do Thunderforge's red-team assessments identify weaknesses that experienced military planners identify? Does it identify weaknesses that human planners miss? (2) Wargame outcome prediction — does Thunderforge's COA assessment correlate with actual wargame outcomes (Track 2 validating Track 1)? (3) Time-to-analysis — how much faster is Thunderforge than the equivalent human staff process? (4) Coverage — what percentage of plan elements did the AI analyze vs. what percentage is typically analyzed in human planning? (5) Safety metrics — rate of hallucinated assessments, escalation bias incidents, ROE-compliance failures. (6) User trust and usability — do commanders find the AI analyses useful, trustworthy, and actionable? INDOPACOM tabletop exercises (June 2025) provide initial evaluation data. No public benchmark results are available.
mapping_strength: Thunderforge maps directly to the JORDAN pipeline's architecture — it is arguably the closest existing system to what JORDAN aims to build, making it a critical reference implementation. (1) Multi-agent architecture: specialized AI agents analyzing different aspects of a problem in parallel, with a fusion layer synthesizing results — this is the exact architecture of a multi-agent research and planning pipeline. (2) Human-on-the-loop: AI agents generate options and analyses; human approves critical decisions — this is JORDAN's approval_gate pattern. (3) Red-teaming function: AI agents stress-testing plans — this maps to JORDAN's risk_assessment node, potentially extended with adversarial validation. (4) Iterative refinement: plan → analyze → revise → re-analyze — this is JORDAN's replan loop. (5) Domain decomposition: specialist agents for different knowledge domains — this maps to JORDAN's potential for multiple specialist agents (legal, security, performance) in addition to the generalist research/planning agents. (6) Traceability and audit: Thunderforge's emphasis on chain-of-evidence and decision logging provides a template for JORDAN's traceability requirements. (7) Wargaming as validation: Thunderforge's use of simulation to validate plans maps to JORDAN's potential use of sandboxed testing environments to validate generated code or architectural decisions.
gaps: Several aspects of Thunderforge do not transfer directly to JORDAN. (1) Classified environment: Thunderforge operates on classified networks with military-specific data and protocols; JORDAN operates in civilian/software domains with different data, constraints, and security requirements. (2) Scale of investment: Thunderforge is a multimillion-dollar DOD program with dedicated infrastructure; JORDAN is a research pipeline. (3) Domain specificity: Thunderforge's agents are designed for military planning; their domain models, knowledge bases, and evaluation criteria don't transfer to software development or general problem-solving. (4) Maturity: Thunderforge is at MVP stage; its reliability, safety, and operational effectiveness are not yet proven — JORDAN cannot simply adopt Thunderforge patterns without independent validation. (5) Adversarial assumption: Thunderforge assumes an intelligent adversary; most JORDAN problem domains are cooperative or indifferent, making the red-teaming and wargaming metaphors potentially over-engineered. (6) Organizational scale: Thunderforge is designed for combatant command staffs of hundreds to thousands; JORDAN may need to operate at much smaller scale (individual developer, small team). (7) Cost structure: Thunderforge's cloud-scale LLM infrastructure may not be economically viable for non-DOD applications.
adaptation_required: To apply Thunderforge lessons to the Jordan pipeline: (1) Adopt the multi-specialist-agent architecture: deploy specialized agents for different analysis dimensions (security, performance, legal compliance, architecture) that operate in parallel on the same plan or code and fuse their assessments. (2) Implement the 'human-on-the-loop' gate: AI generates analyses and recommendations; human approves before significant actions (code deployment, infrastructure changes, plan execution). (3) Build a red-teaming agent: an agent whose sole function is to find weaknesses in plans and code — the adversarial validator that stress-tests before deployment. (4) Implement confidence scoring: every AI-generated assessment should include a confidence score, and low-confidence assessments should be flagged for human review. (5) Build a wargaming/simulation capability: for plans that involve system changes, run sandboxed simulations to validate the plan before execution (analogous to Track 2). (6) Implement chain-of-evidence traceability: every plan, decision, and analysis should be traceable to its evidence basis. (7) Monitor for AI-specific failure modes: hallucination, bias, overconfidence — implement the CMU SEI-style evaluation framework within JORDAN. (8) The Thunderforge model is the most mature reference architecture available for a multi-agent, human-supervised problem-solving pipeline — JORDAN should treat it as a design template, adapting the domain-specific elements for software and general problem-solving contexts.
analogous_jordan_node: plan (primary), risk_assessment (primary), approval_gate (primary), execute (secondary), synthesize (secondary). Thunderforge's core functions — AI-augmented planning, red-teaming/risk assessment, and human approval gating — map to JORDAN's plan, risk_assessment, and approval_gate nodes. The wargaming function maps to execute (sandboxed validation execution) and the fusion layer maps to synthesize.
validation_context: Thunderforge is in early-stage validation: (1) MVP tested with INDOPACOM in a tabletop exercise, June 2025 — initial operational validation. (2) Designed for deployment to INDOPACOM and EUCOM as initial operational users. (3) Integration with DARPA SAFE-SiM architecture for simulation validation. (4) CMU SEI providing independent evaluation of AI trustworthiness. (5) The system has NOT been validated in real-world operational planning for actual combat operations — it is a prototype undergoing operational testing. (6) The underlying LLM technology (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) has been independently studied for military-relevant behaviors (escalation bias, planning quality), providing partial validation of the AI components. (7) Thunderforge's validation context is thinner than the doctrinal military frameworks (AAR, JTC, F2T2EA, FRAGO) because it is a new program — its empirical record is weeks, not decades. (8) The program's validation strategy is explicitly iterative: deploy → test → learn → improve → redeploy.
known_failures: Known failure modes and risks identified in early development: (1) LLM hallucination — the system can produce logically coherent but factually wrong analyses; a warship being routed 'plowing through Australia' is a documented example from adversarial testing. (2) Escalation bias — studies have shown LLMs tend toward escalatory military recommendations; DeepSeek V3, Qwen2, and Llama 8B showed this bias strongly, while GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet were more restrained (the latter two were selected for the program). (3) Overconfidence — AI agents may present low-confidence analyses with high-confidence language, misleading the human commander. (4) Data poisoning — an adversary that knows Thunderforge's architecture could seed disinformation that the AI agents incorporate into their analyses. (5) Degraded performance under EW/Cyber — in real combat, the data feeding Thunderforge may be corrupted by adversary action, and peacetime performance may not reflect wartime degradation. (6) Country-specific bias — models showed varying interventionist/escalatory biases toward different nations (less escalatory toward Russia and China than toward the U.S. or U.K. in some studies). (7) Automation bias — human commanders may develop inappropriate trust in AI analyses, failing to apply their own judgment critically (the 'computer says so' effect). (8) Brittleness — the system may perform well on scenarios similar to its training data but fail catastrophically on novel scenarios that a human would recognize as anomalous. (9) OODA disruption — integrating AI into the planning process changes the command rhythm in ways that may not be fully understood; the human organization may not be adapted to the AI-augmented tempo.
where_still_used: Active development and deployment: (1) Defense Innovation Unit (DIU) — program management and contracting. (2) INDOPACOM — initial operational user; MVP testing June 2025. (3) EUCOM — second operational user; deployment expected 2025-2026. (4) Scale AI — prime contractor developing the agentic AI platform. (5) Microsoft — providing LLM infrastructure and cloud services. (6) Anduril — providing Lattice platform for modeling, simulation, and situational awareness. (7) CMU Software Engineering Institute — AI trustworthiness evaluation and safety framework development. (8) Planned expansion to all 11 U.S. combatant commands (2026-2028). (9) Planned NATO and Five Eyes integration (post-2028). (10) The program is actively growing — it is the DOD's flagship AI-agent program and is likely to receive continued funding and expansion.
